items.question_text: Under 2-3-1, when correcting or updating altitude information on a flight progress strip, which of the following is the correct procedure?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-1a item 1, controllers must not erase or overwrite any item. They must use an 'X' to delete a climb/descend and maintain arrow, an at-or-above/below symbol, a cruise symbol, and unwanted altitude information. The new altitude is written immediately adjacent and within the same space.

choices.text: Erase the old altitude and write the new one in its place.
Use an 'X' to delete unwanted altitude information and write the new altitude adjacent within the same space.
Draw a horizontal line through the old altitude immediately.
Write the new altitude in a separate space and circle the old one.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-1a item 2, when may a horizontal line be drawn through an altitude being vacated?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-1a item 2, the horizontal line is drawn only after the aircraft has reported or is observed (valid Mode C) leaving the altitude. It must not be drawn before the aircraft actually vacates.

choices.text: As soon as the climb/descent clearance is issued.
After the aircraft has reported or is observed (valid Mode C) leaving the altitude.
At any time during the handoff process.
Only when the aircraft reaches the newly assigned altitude.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-1c, altitude information may be written in thousands of feet (e.g., 5,000 as '5') provided the procedure is _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-1c, the abbreviated altitude convention requires authorization by the facility manager and definition in a facility directive.

choices.text: authorized by the facility manager and defined in a facility directive

items.question_text: Under 2-3-1, when is a slant line through the number zero required on flight progress strips?
items.explanation: Per the 2-3-1 note, a slant line through zero and underline of 's' are required only when there is reason to believe the lack of these markings could lead to misunderstanding. However, a slant line through zero is required on ALL weather data.

choices.text: On all flight progress strip entries, without exception.
Only on weather data; on other entries, only when lack of marking could lead to misunderstanding.
Only when the strip is handwritten rather than machine-generated.
Never; it is optional in all cases.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2a, Block 20 of the en route flight progress strip contains _____ information.
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-1, Block 20 records altitude information in hundreds of feet, though it may be written in thousands of feet when authorized by the facility manager and defined in a facility directive.

choices.text: altitude

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2a, when is Block 20a REQUIRED (not optional) for recording RA events?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-1 Block 20a, its use is OPTIONAL when voice recorders are operational but REQUIRED when voice recorders are not operating and strips are being used at the facility. The entry format is 'RA' followed by a climb or descent arrow and the time in hhmm.

choices.text: When voice recorders are not operating and strips are being used at the facility.
For every RA event at all times.
Only when the pilot requests documentation.
Only during hours of darkness.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2a, Block 20a is used to record reported RA events. What is the correct entry format?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-1 Block 20a, the format is the letters 'RA' followed by a climb or descent arrow (if reported) and the time in hhmm when the event is reported.

choices.text: 'TCAS' followed by the altitude and time.
'RA' followed by a climb or descent arrow (if reported) and the time (hhmm).
'RES ADVISORY' followed by the aircraft identification.
'TCAS RA' followed by the bearing and range.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2c, facility air traffic managers may authorize optional use of which blocks for point out information, radar vector information, speed adjustment information, or transfer of control data?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-2c, the seven blocks optionally available are 13, 14, 14a, 22, 23, 24, and 28. This allows facilities to repurpose these fields for supplemental control data.

choices.text: All blocks are mandatory; none can be repurposed.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-3a, Block 4 of the ATOP oceanic flight progress strip contains reduced separation flags. Which of the following is NOT one of the available indicators?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-2 Block 4, the available indicators are: M (Mach Number Technique), R (Reduced MNT), D or 3 (Distance-based longitudinal separation using 50 NM or 30 NM), and W (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum / RVSM). RNAV is not listed as a separation flag in this block.

choices.text: M — Mach Number Technique (MNT).
D — Distance-based longitudinal separation using 50 NM.
W — Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM).
N — RNAV capability.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-3a, Block 7 of the ATOP strip displays the reported flight level. Reports are displayed in which order of preference?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-2 Block 7, reports from Mode C, ADS, or position reports are displayed in that order of preference: Mode C first, then ADS, then position reports.

choices.text: ADS, Mode C, position reports.
Mode C, ADS, position reports.
Position reports, ADS, Mode C.
All sources are given equal preference.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-3a, in the event of a catastrophic system failure, the ATOP system will _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-3a, the ATOP system displays information on electronic flight progress strips and, in the event of a catastrophic system failure, will print flight progress strips with data in the corresponding numbered spaces.

choices.text: print flight progress strips with data in the corresponding numbered spaces

items.question_text: Under 2-3-4, Block 8 of the terminal flight progress strip records the estimated time of arrival at the _____.
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-3 Block 8, this field records the estimated time of arrival at the coordination fix.

choices.text: coordination fix

items.question_text: Under 2-3-4d, air traffic managers at automated terminal radar facilities may waive flight progress strip usage provided all conditions are met. Which is NOT a required condition?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-4d, the five conditions are: (1) backup systems such as multiple radar sites/systems utilized, (2) local procedures documented in a facility directive covering departure/arrival/overflight/nonradar transition/ESL transition, (3) no misunderstanding from no-strip usage, (4) unused strips/forms/notepads provided for controller use, (5) revert to strip usage if backup systems unavailable. 'Fully digital CPDLC environment' is not a condition.

choices.text: Backup systems such as multiple radar sites/systems are utilized.
Local procedures are documented in a facility directive.
A fully digital CPDLC communication environment must be in place.
Facilities must revert to strip usage if backup systems are not available.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-4, Block 8A of the terminal strip (used when voice recorders are NOT operational) records reported RA events. How is this different from the en route strip's Block 20a?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-4 TBL 2-3-3, Block 8A uses the same format as 2-3-2 Block 20a: the letters 'RA' followed by a climb or descent arrow if reported, and the time (hhmm). Both are required when voice recorders are not operational. The recording format is functionally identical.

choices.text: The terminal strip uses a different RA format with latitude/longitude.
There is no meaningful difference; both use the same RA format and trigger condition.
The en route strip does not record RA events at all.
The terminal strip only records RA events during approach phase.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-5, aircraft identity entries may not exceed _____ alphanumeric characters.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-5, aircraft identity is indicated using combinations not to exceed seven alphanumeric characters for all civil, military, and special use identifiers.

choices.text: seven

items.question_text: Under 2-3-5a, when identifying equipment interchange flights on flight progress strips, whose company name should be used?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-5a, controllers must use the operating air carrier's company name in identifying equipment interchange flights, not the owning company.

choices.text: The aircraft owner's company name.
The operating air carrier's company name.
The manufacturer's name followed by the registration.
A generic 'interchange' identifier.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-5b, military aircraft identities use prefixes indicating branch of service and/or mission followed by the last _____ digits of the serial number (or last _____ for CFC and CTG).
items.explanation: Per 2-3-5b item 1, the format uses the last 5 digits of the serial number, except for CFC (Canadian Forces) and CTG (Canadian Coast Guard) which use the last 4 digits.

choices.text: 5, 4 digits

items.question_text: Under 2-3-5, when the President is on a commercial aircraft, the aircraft identity is _____.
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-8, when the President is aboard a commercial aircraft, the identifier is EXEC1. For the Vice President on a commercial aircraft, it is EXEC2. Family members use EXEC1F or EXEC2F respectively.

choices.text: EXEC1

items.question_text: Under 2-3-5a, the letter 'L' is used in aircraft identification to indicate _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-5a, the letter 'L' is used for MEDEVAC aircraft. However, the note clarifies that 'L' is not to be used for air carrier/air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft. The example 'LN751B' shows 'L' used in a civil registration context.

choices.text: MEDEVAC

items.question_text: Under 2-3-6, what reference provides the approved aircraft type designators for flight progress strips?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-6, approved aircraft type designators are specified in FAA Order 7360.1, Aircraft Type Designators.

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Appendix A.
FAA Order 7360.1, Aircraft Type Designators.
FAA Order JO 7340.2, Contractions.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-7, what suffix identifies USAF/USN undergraduate student pilots on the flight plan, and where must it NOT be used?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-7, the letter 'Z' must be included as a suffix to the aircraft identification in the flight plan. However, this suffix must not be used in ground-to-air communication.

choices.text: Suffix 'S' on the flight plan; not used on ATC frequency.
Suffix 'Z' on the flight plan; not used in ground-to-air communication.
Suffix 'U' on the flight plan; not used in the remarks section.
Suffix 'T' on the flight plan; not used on handoff coordination.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-7, why might controllers expect revised clearance requests from USAF/USN undergraduate student pilots?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-7, these pilots are normally restricted to flight in VFR conditions, and the note clarifies that USAF solo students who have passed an instrument certification check may penetrate cloud layers in climb or descent only. Revised clearance requests to avoid clouds in level flight can still be expected.

choices.text: Because they are normally restricted to flight in VFR conditions.
Because they operate under special VFR clearances only.
Because their aircraft have limited fuel endurance.
Because they are required to practice emergency procedures.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-8a, to change an aircraft equipment suffix, what must be modified?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-8a, the suffix is generated by automation using ICAO equipment codes. To change a suffix, the CNS equipment codes must be modified, letting automation translate them into the proper suffix. Directly changing the suffix may cause unintended changes (see note).

choices.text: The CNS equipment codes in the ICAO flight plan.
The suffix directly by preceding it with a slant on the strip.
The transponder code assigned to the aircraft.
The aircraft type designator in the flight plan.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-8b, which systems are best suited for making changes to equipment codes in an ICAO flight plan?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-8b, ERAM and ATOP are best suited for making changes to the equipment codes. For FDIO entries, if uncertain of the format, the change should be verbally coordinated with the appropriate en route facility.

choices.text: FDIO and STARS.
ERAM and ATOP.
FDP2000 and OFDPS.
ASDE-X and AMASS.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-8, a GNSS-equipped aircraft will have an equipment suffix of _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-8d, GNSS-equipped aircraft have an equipment suffix of /G, /L, /S, or /V. These can be determined by ICAO flight plan readout (verifying filed 'G' in ICAO equipment list) or by verifying with the pilot.

choices.text: /G, /L, /S, or /V

items.question_text: Under 2-3-8, which equipment suffixes are for ATC use only and not published in the Aeronautical Information Manual?
items.explanation: Per the 2-3-8 note, /H and /O are intended for ATC use only and are not published in the AIM. /H indicates failed transponder and /O indicates failed Mode C.

choices.text: /H and /O.
/X and /T.
/G and /L.
/A and /W.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-9, which clearance symbol indicates that holding instructions have been included in the aircraft's original clearance?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-9a, the symbol 'H' at the clearance limit is used when holding instructions have been included in the original clearance. Detailed holding information follows the dash when holding differs from the established pattern.

choices.text: Symbol 'F'.
Symbol 'H'.
Symbol 'O'.
Symbol 'D'.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-9, the symbols 'F' or 'O' at the clearance limit indicate that _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-9b, 'F' or 'O' are used to indicate the clearance limit when a delay is not anticipated. This contrasts with 'H' which is used when holding/delay is anticipated.

choices.text: a delay is not anticipated

items.question_text: Under 2-3-9, when must detailed holding information be shown following the dash after the 'H' symbol?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-9a, detailed holding information (turns, leg lengths, etc.) must be shown following the dash when holding differs from the established pattern for the fix.

choices.text: Always, for every holding clearance.
When holding differs from the established pattern for the fix.
Only when requested by the receiving facility.
Only for oceanic holdings.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, which clearance abbreviation means 'Cleared to the fix'?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-11, the abbreviation 'F' means 'Cleared to the fix'. Other common abbreviations: A = Cleared to airport, C = ATC clears, CAF = Cleared as filed, D = Cleared to depart from the fix, H = Cleared to hold.

choices.text: A.
F.
H.
C.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, which clearance abbreviation means 'Cleared through' (for landing and takeoff through intermediate point)?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-11, 'T' means 'Cleared through' for landing and takeoff through intermediate point. This allows an aircraft to land and then take off through an intermediate point in its route.

choices.text: C.
X.
T.
V.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, which miscellaneous abbreviation indicates a VOR approach?
items.explanation: Per TBL 2-3-12, 'VR' is the abbreviation for VOR approach. Other approach abbreviations include: ILS, GPS, RNAV, VA (visual approach), SA (surveillance approach), TA (TACAN approach), NDB, BC (back course).

choices.text: VR

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, locally approved identifiers may be used within a facility but must NOT be used on _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-10b, locally approved identifiers are restricted to use within a facility only and must not be used on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.

choices.text: teletypewriter or interphone circuits
